does: This paper investigates whether different large language models learn analogous feature spaces despite learning different individual feature representations. The authors employ sparse autoencoders (SAEs) to disentangle polysemantic neurons into interpretable features corresponding to distinct concepts, then develop methods to quantify similarity between feature spaces across models. Rather than asking whether models learn identical features, they ask whether the geometric structure and dimensionality of the spaces spanned by SAE features are similar across different LLMs. The core contribution is formalizing and providing empirical evidence for what they call Analogous Feature Universality—demonstrating that even when individual SAE features differ between models, the underlying spaces they inhabit share fundamental structural properties.
connects: This work builds on the broader Universality Hypothesis in mechanistic interpretability, which posits that different LLMs converge toward similar internal representations. It extends prior research investigating whether models learn identical features by addressing a fundamental challenge: polysemanticity, where single neurons activate across multiple unrelated concepts. The use of sparse autoencoders to decompose neurons into interpretable features builds on recent SAE-based mechanistic interpretability research. This paper connects to work on feature learning across model architectures and sizes, responding to the limitation that previous feature comparison studies could not adequately handle the complexity of neuron polysemanticity. It positions itself within ongoing efforts to understand what properties of LLM representations are truly universal versus model-specific.
enables: This work enables more robust generalization of mechanistic interpretability techniques across different LLM architectures and scales. By establishing that feature spaces are structurally similar even when individual features differ, researchers can develop interpretation methods that exploit universal properties without requiring model-specific retraining. The framework could facilitate transfer of safety techniques, alignment methods, and interpretability tools across diverse models. Future work could use these insights to design interventions that operate at the feature space level rather than individual feature level, potentially making mechanistic techniques more scalable and robust. This foundation also opens investigation into which structural properties of feature spaces are truly universal and why, deepening understanding of how different training processes converge to similar representational geometries.
